flags.sentence: Under the stated low-scale condition, there is an open interval of pseudoinverse weights in which the spread covariance is the unique relaxed optimum, is not implementable, and yields strictly positive implementability loss.
flags.fix: Under two-block homophily, the stated low-scale condition, odd community size, and the stated robustness range, there is an open interval of pseudoinverse weights in which the spread covariance is the unique relaxed optimum, is not implementable, and yields strictly positive implementability loss.

flags.sentence: Away from that locus, increasing \(\kappa\) moves relaxed minimizers toward \(I_n\), but no finite positive \(\kappa\) makes \(I_n\) itself optimal.
flags.fix: Away from that locus, relaxed minimizers converge entrywise to \(I_n\) as \(\kappa\to\infty\), but no finite positive \(\kappa\) makes \(I_n\) itself optimal.

flags.sentence: Unequal block sizes would change both the spectral reduction and the block-sum lattice.
flags.fix: The present results do not cover unequal block sizes, where both the spectral reduction and the block-sum lattice would require new arguments.

flags.sentence: With more than two communities, the block elliptope would no longer be described by the three coordinates \((x,y,z_{\mathrm{sp}})\), and implementability would depend on a higher-dimensional collection of community sums.
flags.fix: The present results do not cover more than two communities; the three-coordinate reduction \((x,y,z_{\mathrm{sp}})\) and the implementability characterization would require modification.

flags.sentence: In the two-block homophily model, the relaxed block elliptope \(\mathcal E_m^{\mathrm{blk}}\) is not only a computational device; it is also a benchmark against which exact implementability by a balanced \(\{\pm1\}\) assignment law can be measured.
flags.fix: In the two-block homophily model, the relaxed block elliptope \(\mathcal E_m^{\mathrm{blk}}\) is not only a computational device; it is also a benchmark against which exact implementability by a sign-symmetric, block-exchangeable \(\{\pm1\}\) assignment law can be measured.

weaknesses: The design objective F_{r,κ} (Definition 'design-objective', Section 2) is the paper's central object but each of its four terms gets only a one-line gloss; there is no derivation tying it to a design-based estimator variance, robustness criterion, or MSE under interference, so the reader cannot tell what the 'implementability price' Δ actually buys econometrically. Fix: add a paragraph deriving or explicitly citing the source of each term (especially the Schatten-2 penalty and the L†-weighted term) from a treatment-effect variance/robustness objective.

The closest cited work, thiyageswaran2026 (covariance-based design for network experiments), is invoked three times (intro, block-elliptope, relaxation) but never distinguished in scope or results; the positioning paragraphs otherwise drop citations in generic clusters. Fix: add one-two sentences in the introduction stating precisely how this paper's exact two-block implementability characterization relates to and departs from that covariance-design formulation.

The 'two equal communities' restriction is hard-coded into Definition 'two-block-graph' and the whole spectral reduction, yet it is never flagged as a substantive modeling assumption—it surfaces only obliquely in the extensions section ('unequal block sizes... would require new arguments'). Fix: explicitly state in Section 2 that equal community size is a maintained structural restriction, not a normalization, and note what breaks without it.

The paper offers no numerical or worked illustration of its headline positive-loss result: for the Positive-Gap Window (Theorem 'gap-window') a concrete choice of (m,a,b) exhibiting the interval (r_gap^-, r_gap^+) and a nonzero Δ would let readers gauge whether the gap is ever quantitatively non-negligible, especially since the text concedes the parity truncation d_m=2/m shrinks with m. Fix: add a short numerical example (or small table) instantiating the gap window and the sharp loss ρ_⋆.

Appendix A opens by referring to 'the formal results in Sections~\ref{obj:thm:gap-window} and~\ref{obj:thm:sharp-rho-star}', citing two theorem labels as if they were section numbers; this is a cross-reference error that will render as wrong numbers. Fix: change to 'Theorems~\ref{...}'.

The abstract is a single dense block that front-loads unexplained technical loci (e.g., 'a+3b=2m and r=2b(a+b)', 'spectral coordinates (m/(m-1),0,0)') before the reader has any of the setup, hurting accessibility for the econometrics audience it targets. Fix: trim the abstract to the three qualitative findings (cut-corner exactness, iid knife-edge, odd-parity positive loss + exact formula) and move the explicit loci into the body.

Abstract: it is too theorem-internal and symbol-heavy for a first pass; define less, state the econometric takeaway first, and move phrases like “affine locus \(a+3b=2m\)” and “active-set formula” to the introduction summary.

Introduction/Setup: the objective \(F_{r,\kappa}\) is described term-by-term but not derived from a design risk, estimator variance, or robustness problem; add a paragraph explaining what estimand or worst-case criterion makes these four terms jointly natural.

Claims around verification: “machine-checked” may read as certifying the whole paper, while Appendix B says prose, modeling interpretation, and primitive matrix facts are not verified; add that qualification near the first Lean-verification claim, not only in the appendix.

Positioning: the literature discussion is broad but not very diagnostic about what prior covariance-based network design work proves versus what this paper adds; add 2-3 precise contrasts, especially with \citet{thiyageswaran2026} and standard SDP/correlation relaxation literature.

Theorem presentation: Sections 3-4 repeat long formal hypotheses and conclusions in prose, making the main economic message harder to see; add short “Interpretation” paragraphs before or after each theorem and move some computational details to a boxed recipe or appendix.

Assumption discussion: equal block sizes, complete two-block weighted graph, sign symmetry without fixed treated count, and the scaling \(a/m,b/m\) are acknowledged but not fully stress-tested; add a compact limitations paragraph in Setup explaining which empirical designs these assumptions exclude and why they are mathematically essential here.
